title: Do Androids Dream of Electric Fences? Safety-Aware Reinforcement Learning with Latent Shielding

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DO ANDROIDS DREAM OF ELECTRIC FENCES? SAFETY-AWARE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH LATENT SHIELDING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The growing trend of fledgling reinforcement learning systems making their way into real-world applications has been accompanied by growing concerns for their safety and robustness. In recent years, a variety of approaches have been put forward to address the challenges of safety-aware reinforcement learning; however, these methods often either require a handcrafted model of the environment to be provided beforehand, or that the environment is relatively simple and lowdimensional. We present a novel approach to safety-aware deep reinforcement learning in high-dimensional environments called _latent shielding_ . Latent shielding leverages internal representations of the environment learnt by model-based agents to “imagine” future trajectories and avoid those deemed unsafe. We experimentally demonstrate that this approach leads to improved adherence to formallydefined safety specifications.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The steady trickle of reinforcement learning (RL) systems making their way out of the lab and into the real world has cast a spotlight on the safety and robustness of RL agents. The motivation behind this should be relatively easy to grasp: when training an agent in real-world settings, it is desirable that some states are never reached as they could, for instance, cause permanent damage to the hardware the agent is controlling. We can thus informally define the notion of _safety-aware RL_ in terms of the classical RL setup with the added requirement that the number of _unsafe_ states visited be minimised. Under this definition, however, it has been found that many state-of-the-art RL algorithms unnecessarily enter unsafe states despite safe alternatives being available and there being a positive correlation between avoiding such states and reward (Giacobbe et al., 2021).

The field of safety-aware RL encompasses a multitude of approaches ranging from constrained policy optimisation (Chow et al., 2017; Achiam et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020) to safety critics (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Thananjeyan et al., 2021) to meta-learning (Turchetta et al., 2020). In this work, we focus on a particular family of approaches known as _shielding_ (Alshiekh et al., 2018; Anderson et al., 2020; Giacobbe et al., 2021; ElSayed-Aly et al., 2021; Pranger et al., 2021). Central to shielding is the notion of a _shield_ , a filter that checks actions proposed by the agent’s existing policy with reference to a model of the environment’s dynamics and some formal safety specification. The shield overrides actions that may lead to an unsafe state using some other safe (but by no means optimal) policy. A key advantage of many shielding approaches is that the resulting _shielded_ policies are formally verifiable; however, a shortcoming is that they require a model of environmental dynamics - typically handcrafted - to be provided in advance. Providing such a model may prove difficult for complex real-world environments, with inaccuracies and human biases creeping into handcrafted models.

In this work, we propose a safe RL agent that makes uses of _latent shielding_ , an approach to shielding in environments where a formally-specified dynamics model is not available in advance. At an intuitive level, the agent uses a data-driven approach to learn its own latent world model (a component of which is a dynamics model) which is then leveraged by a shield. The shield then uses the agent’s model to “imagine” trajectories arising from different actions, forcing the agent to avoid

those it foresees leading to unsafe states. In addition, the agent can be trained within its own latent world model thus reducing the number of safety violations seen during training.

**Contributions** The main contribution of this work is a framework for shielding agents in complex, stochastic and high-dimensional environments without knowledge of environmental dynamics _a priori_ . We further introduce a new method to aid exploration when training shielded agents. Though our framework loses the formal safety guarantees associated with traditional symbolic shielding approaches, our experiments illustrate that latent shielding reduces unsafe behaviour during training and achieves testing performance comparable to previous symbolic approaches.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

In this section, we cover some relevant background topics. We begin by introducing our problem setup for safety-aware RL and give an overview of the specification language used in this work. This is followed by an outline of the latent world model we make use of in this work as well as a discussion on shielding.

## 2.1 PROBLEM SETUP

We consider an agent interacting with an environment _E_ modelled as a _partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP)_ with states _s ∈SE_ , observations _ot ∈OE_ , agent-generated actions _at ∈AE_ and scalar rewards _rt ∈_ R over discrete time steps _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1 _, ..., T −_ 1]. We assume the environment has been augmented with a labelling function _L[φ] E_[:] _[S][E][→{][safe, unsafe][}]_[that,] at each time step, informs us whether a _violation_ has occurred with respect to some formal safety specification _φ_ . For the avoidance of doubt, we define a violation to have occurred whenever _φ_ does not hold. This is a weaker assumption than previous works in shielding (which assume access to an abstraction of the environment) and can be thought as a secondary safety-focused reward function with a binary output. Intuitively, the goal of the agent is to learn a policy _π_ that maximises its expected cumulative reward while minimising the number of violations of _φ_ .

## 2.2 SYNTACTICALLY CO-SAFE LINEAR TEMPORAL LOGIC

In this work, we use _syntactically co-safe Linear Temporal Logic (scLTL)_ (Kupferman & Vardi, 2001) as our specification language. Valid scLTL formulae over some set of atomic propositions _AP_ can be constructed according to the following grammar:

_φ_ ::= _true | d | ¬d | φ ∨ φ | φ ∧ φ | ⃝φ | φ ∪ φ | ⋄φ_ (1)

where _d ∈ AP_ , _¬ (negation)_ , _∨ (disjunction)_ , _∧ (conjunction)_ are the familiar operators from propositional logic, and _⃝ (next)_ , _∪ (until)_ and _⋄ (eventually)_ are temporal operators. We can monitor a co-safe LTL specification using a technique known as _progression_ (Bacchus & Kabanza, 2000).

## 2.3 RECURRENT STATE-SPACE MODELS

We refer to the predictive model of an environment maintained by a model-based agent as its _world model_ . World models can be learnt from experience and be used both as a substitute for the environment during training (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2021) and for planning at run-time (Hafner et al., 2019b). Though many realisations of the notion of a world model exist, the world model used in this work is based on the _recurrent state-space model (RSSM)_ proposed by Hafner et al. (2019b).

An RSSM is composed of three key components: a latent dynamics model, a reward model, and an observation model. These components act on compact states formed from the concatenation of a deterministic latent state _ht_ and stochastic latent state _zt_ .

**Latent Dynamics Model** The latent dynamics model is made up of a number of smaller models. First, the _recurrent model ht_ = _f_ ( _ht−_ 1 _, zt−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) is used to compute the deterministic latent state based on the previous compact state and action. From _ht_ and the current observation _ot_ , a

2

distribution _q_ ( _zt|ht, ot_ ) over posterior stochastic latent states _zt_ is computed by the _representation model_ . At the same time, a distribution _p_ (ˆ _zt|ht_ ) over prior stochastic latent states _z_ ˆ _t_ is computed by the _transition model_ , based only on _ht_ . During training, the transition model attempts to minimise the _Kullback Leibler (KL)_ divergence between the prior and posterior stochastic latent state distributions. In doing this, the RSSM learns to predict future latent states (using the recurrent and transition models) without access to future observations.

**Observation Model** The _observation model_ computes the distribution _p_ (ˆ _ot|ht, zt_ ) over observations _o_ ˆ _t_ for a particular state. Though not strictly needed, the observation model can prove useful for visualising predicted future states and providing a richer training signal.
**Reward Model** The _reward model_ computes the distribution _p_ (ˆ _rt|ht, zt_ ) over rewards _r_ ˆ _t_ for a particular state.

In practice, the distributions _p_ and _q_ are implemented with neural networks _pθ_ and _qθ_ respectively, parameterised by some set of parameters _θ_ . These latent dynamics models define a fully-observable _Markov decision process (MDP)_ as the latent states in the agent’s own internal model can always be observed by the agent (Hafner et al., 2019a). We denote the state space of this MDP (comprised of compact latent states) as _SI_ .

## 2.4 SHIELDING

The _classical_ formulation of shielding in RL is given by Alshiekh et al. (2018). It assumes access to two ingredients: an LTL safety specification and _abstraction_ (a MDP model of the environment that captures the aspects of the environment relevant for planning ahead with respect to the safety specification). These ingredients are used to construct a formally verifiable reactive system that monitors the agent’s actions, overriding those which lead to violation states.

Proposed by Giacobbe et al. (2021), _bounded prescience shielding (BPS)_ avoids the need for handcrafted abstractions by exploiting the fact that some agents are trained in computer simulations. The shield operates by leveraging access to the program underlying the simulation to look ahead into future states within some finite horizon. Using BPS over classical shielding does, however, come with a few disadvantages. Firstly, it requires access to the simulation at run-time which may prove difficult to provide (especially in cases where running the simulation is computationally expensive). Moreover, an agent using BPS, even when starting from a safe state, can find itself entering unsafe states in cases where the number of steps between a violation being caused by an action and the violation state itself exceeds the shield’s look-ahead horizon. This is not the case for classical shielding which resembles BPS with an infinite horizon.

## 2.5 BOUNDED SAFETY

The notion of safety used by BPS is defined over MDPs. For an arbitrary MDP with states _S_ and actions _A_ , a _bounded trajectory ρ_ of length _H_ is a sequence of states and actions _s_ 0 _−→a_ 0 _s_ 1 _−→a_ 1 _an−_ 1 _. . . −−−→ sn_ comprised of no more than _H_ states and with the final state _sn_ either being a terminal state or _n_ = _H −_ 1. We further denote the set of all finite trajectories starting from some arbitrary state _s ∈S_ by _ϱ_ ( _s_ ) and the set of all bounded trajectories of length _H_ that start from _s_ by _ϱH_ ( _s_ ).

We say a bounded trajectory _ρ_ of length _H_ satisfies _H-bounded safety_ with respect to safety specification _φ_ , written _SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ), if and only if for all _si ∈ ρ_ , _L[φ] E_[(] _[s][i]_[) =] _[ safe]_[.][Moreover, we can extend] the notion of _H_ -bounded safety over the set of policies: a policy _π_ is _H-bounded safe_ with respect to _φ_ , denoted as _SH_ ( _π, φ_ ), if and only if for all s _∈S_ ,

- either there exists some _ρ ∈ ϱH_ ( _s_ ) such that _SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ) and _π_ ( _s_ 0) = _a_ 0;

- or for all _ρ ∈ ϱH_ ( _s_ ), _¬SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ) _._

In other words, the policy will choose a safe trajectory as long as one exists. Finally, we formally define a violation of _φ_ to be _inevitable_ in state _s_ 0 _∈S_ if and only if for all _ρ ∈ ϱ_ ( _s_ 0), _¬SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ).

## 3 SHIELDED DREAMS

We introduce the notion of _latent shielding_ , a novel class of shielding approaches that replace abstractions used by shields with learned latent dynamics models thus allowing the enforcement of formal safety specifications while avoiding the need for an explicitly-defined abstraction of the environment. We further introduce the first such approach, _approximate bounded prescience shielding_ , a framework for latent shielding that leverages latent world models learnt by model-based _deep RL (DRL)_ agents. In this work, our model-based agent of choice is Dreamer (Hafner et al., 2021)[1] , which we modify to incorporate shielding into its data collection, training and deployment phases.

## 3.1 SAFETY RSSM

We augment the standard RSSM with a labelling function _L[φ] I_[:] _[ S][I][→{][safe, unsafe][}]_[ which maps] latent states _st ∈SI_ to whether they correspond to states in violation of _φ_ . As with the other models, _L[φ] I_[is implemented with a neural network with a categorical output] _[ l][θ]_[also parameterised by] _[ θ]_[.][This] yields an enhanced RSSM (illustrated in Figure 1) which we will refer to as a _safety RSSM (SRSSM)_ .

The inclusion of the weighting hyperparameter _α_ is motivated by the observation that the number of observations of safe and unsafe states will generally be quite imbalanced. Thus, to prevent predictions by _lθ_ from collapsing to a single class, we assign a greater weight to the less-represented class.

## 3.2 APPROXIMATE BOUNDED PRESCIENCE SHIELDING

We now integrate the SRSSM as part of a latent shielding approach which we shall refer to as _approximate bounded prescience shielding (ABPS)_ . Though ABPS is inspired by BPS, it differs in

two key aspects: (1) we approximate the labelling function _L[φ] E_[and environmental dynamics using an] SRSSM; and (2) we sample a fixed number of potential future trajectories as opposed to exhaustively exploring all possibilities.

Thus, our approach can be thought of as an approximation of some “ideal” bounded prescience shield that uses the true environmental dynamics and labelling function. The advantage of the first difference should be obvious: it enables the shield to learn its own abstraction, removing the need for hand-crafting or access to a digital environment’s underlying program. Why the second difference is advantageous may be slightly less obvious - it’s a heuristic that allows us to increase the horizon _H_ . By directing the sampling of trajectories in accordance with states and actions the policy is biased towards (as opposed to uniformly), it may be possible to achieve comparable performance to a standard BPS in foreseeing unsafe states. The intuition behind this is that by not sampling trajectories the agent is unlikely to take, more of our computational budget can be dedicated into ensuring that the agent’s most likely trajectories are safe. In other words, we will not spend time planning to correct actions that the agent is unlikely to take.

More formally, our shielded policy _π[∗]_ can be written:

where _π_ ( _st_ ) is the agent’s policy without shielding; _st ∈SI_ is some compact latent state; and _πalt_ is an alternative policy that ensures that, if a violation isn’t already inevitable, the agent avoids a predicted unsafe state. In our implementation, _πalt_ simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found. In the event that no safe action is found, the agent takes a random action.

Since the SRSSM models stochasticity, we can sample multiple futures arising from an action being taken and derive probabilistic estimates of whether an action will lead to a violation. In this way, we can estimate the safety of an action taken in a given state by checking whether the probability of a violation occurring (inferred by sampling) exceeds a fixed threshold _ϵ_ (see Algorithm 1). Moreover, the sampling process can, in practice, be augmented to sample a wider range of trajectories (less likely to be taken by the agent) by adding a small amount of noise to actions suggested by the policy during sampling.

3.3 TRAINING REGIME

We extend the training regime proposed in Hafner et al. (2019a) to include the training and application of ABPS. Though the full training procedure is detailed in Algorithm 2, we provide an overview below.

## 3.3.1 OVERVIEW

The training procedure can be split into three phases: data collection, latent world model training and agent training (lines 10-22, 4-6 and 7-8 in Algorithm 2 respectively). These phases are cycled through until convergence.

**Data Collection** In this phase, the agent interacts with the real environment to collect a dataset _D ⊆S × A × O ×_ R _× {_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ of states, actions, observations, rewards and violations with which a latent world model can be learned. At the very start of training, we collect trajectories from _S_ seed episodes using a random policy; at all other times, we use the agent’s shielded policy. An illustration of the components of the agent active during this phase can be found in Figure 4 in Appendix A.

**Latent World Model Training** The goal of this phase is to improve the model of the world so that the policy has an accurate imagined environment to train in. To this end, we train the latent world model with respect to the objective in Equation 2 on _B_ data sequences of length _L_ sampled from _D_ . An illustration of the components of the agent active during this phase can be found in Figure 5 in Appendix A.

**Agent Training** Agent training is composed of two steps. First, starting from states from the same _B_ data sequences from the latent world model training phase, the agent imagines _I_ trajectories of length _H_ with actions chosen from its current policy. Next, the unshielded policy _π_ is updated as in Hafner et al. (2019a; 2021).

## 3.3.2 KEY CHANGES AND CONTRIBUTIONS

We describe and discuss the key changes we have made that differentiate our agent from previous approaches.
**Experience Dataset.** Elements in the experience dataset _D_ now contain a binary variable representing whether a violation has occurred _λt_ .

**Latent Shielding.** Before being sent to the environment _E_ , actions _at_ generated by the unshielded policy _π_ are routed through our newly-proposed latent shield (described in Algorithm 1). The shield returns a new approximately _H_ -bounded safe action _a[′] t_[.]

**Intrinsic Punishment.** Whenever a violation occurs (whether detected by the latent shield or by the environment), we override the environment’s reward function by instead assigning a reward _p <_ 0. This discourages the agent’s unshielded policy _π_ from taking actions that lead to unsafe states through the standard RL setup. Over time, this means that the shield will have to interfere less as _π_ becomes biased away from unsafe states. This is a standard practice in the shielding literature (Alshiekh et al., 2018), however new to the Dreamer family of agents (Hafner et al., 2019a;b; 2021).

**Shield Introduction Schedule** We introduce the novel notion of a _shield introduction schedule_ Π which can enable and disable shielding during training. The rationale behind this is that a shield backed by an inaccurate world model will incorrectly label some safe states as unsafe, and vice versa. In some cases, this may prove detrimental to the training process: suppose _lθ_ happens to be initialised in such a way that all states are labelled as unsafe. As a result, the shield can prevent the agent from exploring the environment and improving its internal model of the environment. This, in turn, can prevent the labelling function from learning to correctly differentiate safe states from unsafe states.

To give the agent time to learn a good world model before restricting exploration through a learned shield, we augment the training procedure with Π which aims to gradually introduce shielding. To our knowledge, this is the first time such a system has been proposed. Though there exist a wide range of possible implementations of Π, in this work we use a simple schedule that seems to work well empirically: start training with shielding disabled and enable shielding once the world model loss begins to plateau (see Appendix H for more details). A detailed comparison of different shield introduction schedules, however, is beyond the scope of this study and left for further work.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we compare our ABPS agent against Dreamer without shielding (Hafner et al., 2019a; 2021), Dreamer with BPS (Giacobbe et al., 2021), and CPO (Achiam et al., 2017). We also empirically investigate some aspects of the internal workings of our agent. A brief overview of the environments used can be found below with more detailed a discussion in Appendix C.

**Visual Grid World** The _Visual Grid World (VGW)_ environment is a simple deterministic navigation benchmark with high-dimensional (64 _×_ 64 _×_ 3) visual observations (see Figure 2) and discrete actions. The agent’s (in green) task is to navigate to randomly-placed targets (in black) while avoiding any unsafe locations (in red). We experiment with both _fixed_ and _procedurally generated_ grids. **Cliff Driver** The _Cliff Driver (CD)_ environment is a symbolic benchmark with stochastic dynamics and continuous actions. The agent controls the forward acceleration of a car and is tasked with driving to the edge of a cliff as quickly as possible without overshooting and falling into the sea. Stochasticity comes from the fact that at each time step there is a probability _pstick_ that car’s controls become “stuck” (Machado et al., 2018).

As can be seen in Table 4, our agent collected more reward at test-time than both the unshielded and BPS agents in the VGW environment (reward curves during training can be seen in Figure 3 with further training curves showcased in Appendix E). Moreover, our agent saw a seven-fold reduction in test-time violations compared to the unshielded agent across both the fixed and procedurally generated environments, averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW. This reduction in training violations is even more dramatic when compared to the model-free CPO agent. Our agent outperformed the other agents at test-time in the most stochastic CD environment ( _pstick_ = 0 _._ 5). This is possibly due to the SRSSM used by our latent shield being better able to capture non-determinism than BPS. Nevertheless, the result is still rather impressive given that the agent using BPS sampled 1024 trajectories at every time step whereas our agent only sampled 20.

## 4.2 OTHER EXPERIMENTS

**Qualitative Evaluation of Learned Latent Dynamics** We evaluate the quality of each agent’s latent dynamics model by observing its trajectory predictions given a particular starting state and action sequence. Each agent is given the same starting observation and predefined sequence of 10 actions. The agents take these actions in their respective latent world models with the states traversed being decoded for inspection. Results can be seen in Appendix G and indicate that, qualitatively, our agent’s decoder performed the best, accurately predicting 10 frames into the future. One possible reason for this observation is that the inclusion of the violation detection objective in the SRSSM encourages the model to focus on accurately capturing violation states. Another potential factor is that the BPS agent never actually enters unsafe states and thus finds it difficult to represent them.

## 5 RELATED WORK AND DISCUSSION

**Latent World Models** Learning latent world models from visual observations has seen growing interest from the RL community (Wahlstr¨om et al., 2015; Watter et al., 2015; Racani`ere et al., 2017; Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2019a;b; Schrittwieser et al., 2020; Hafner et al., 2021). One key trend in the literature is that of training agents in their own learned world models (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2019a; 2021). In this work, we extend the world model formulation used in Hafner et al. (2019a;b; 2021) to encode a notion of safety into state representations.

**Safety By Filtering Actions** Overriding unsafe actions with safe ones has been a popular approach to safety-aware RL. Introduced into the RL scene by Alshiekh et al. (2018), shielding has received much research interest and has seen applications in real-world settings (Nikou et al., 2021). Various works have attempted to address some of its shortcomings of the original formulation. These include allowing the shield to be updated to aid exploration or correct model imperfections (Anderson et al., 2020; Pranger et al., 2021); improving performance in non-deterministic environments (Jansen et al., 2020; Li & Bastani, 2020); and extending shielding to multi-agent RL (ElSayed-Aly et al., 2021). To our knowledge, few works (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Giacobbe et al., 2021) focus on removing the need for handcrafting an abstraction (among the most time-consuming and error-prone aspects of shielding), and only one of them (Giacobbe et al., 2021) achieves this without getting rid of the abstraction altogether, albeit by providing the agent with access to the program that controls the environment.

In contrast, latent shielding tackles the problem head-on by directly learning an abstraction for use by the shield. In this work, the abstraction we use is an SRSSM, which captures stochasticity by design, a useful property for non-deterministic environments. Though our latent shield satisfies an approximation of _H_ -bounded safety (see Section 2.4) with respect to the learned abstraction, its safety with respect to the true environmental dynamics is not guaranteed and instead relies on the fidelity with which the true dynamics are captured. Furthermore, unlike for its formally-verified predecessors, it is a necessary sacrifice that the agent visits unsafe states during training in order to learn a notion of safety (unless, of course, pre-training is possible). Nevertheless, a learned abstraction may be advantageous in settings where handcrafting an abstraction is not feasible and privileged access to some simulation (as in Giacobbe et al. (2021)) cannot be assumed. Moreover, latent shielding may provide a greater degree of explainability over model-free methods which get rid of the abstraction altogether (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021) - if the shield overrides an action, one can reconstruct the imagined unsafe trajectories that led to the interference.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we have presented _latent shielding_ , a new framework for shielding DRL agents without the need for a handcrafted abstraction of the environment. Using this framework, we have designed a novel shield and demonstrated that this method not only leads to improved adherence to safety specifications on two benchmark environments with respect to an unshielded agent, but also works out-of-the-box on both continuous and discrete environments (unlike its predecessor, BPS). Furthermore, we have demonstrated for the first time that shielding at inappropriate times may adversely impact the performance of model-based DRL agents and showed how this phenomenon can be counteracted using our novel notion of _shield introduction schedules_ .

Our work opens several exciting avenues for future work. For instance, this work uses a very simple shield introduction schedule; future work may provide a richer investigation into the properties of different schedules. Moreover, though demonstrating promising empirical results, our realisation of latent shielding loses the formally verifiable safety guarantees enjoyed by many symbolic shielding approaches - whether it is possible to construct a verifiable implementation of latent shielding, or compensate for the loss of formal guarantees, are open problems.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel approach to safe RL that combines learned latent dynamics models with shielding, removing the need for handcrafted environment abstractions. The core idea is timely and the empirical results on two benchmark environments are promising. However, several major issues with the methodology, evaluation, and framing need to be addressed before the paper can be considered for publication at a top venue.

The paper tackles an important problem—scalable safe RL without handcrafted models—and the latent shielding framework is a creative integration of existing ideas. The empirical results show reductions in safety violations, which is encouraging. However, the paper falls short in several critical areas: the safety guarantees are not properly characterized, the evaluation is limited in scope and rigor, and key design choices (e.g., shield horizon, trajectory sampling) lack justification. The contribution is real but incomplete as presented.

Overview issues:

- Unclear and potentially misleading safety guarantees: The paper repeatedly claims that latent shielding 'enforces formal safety specifications' and 'improves adherence' to them, yet it also acknowledges that the approach loses the formal guarantees of symbolic shielding. The actual guarantee is only with respect to the learned abstraction (SRSSM), not the real environment. The paper never quantifies the gap between the learned and true dynamics, nor does it provide any bound on the probability of a real-world violation. This is a critical omission: a reader cannot tell whether the method is merely a heuristic that sometimes works or has a principled safety argument. The paper should either (a) provide a probabilistic safety bound that accounts for model error (e.g., using high-confidence prediction intervals or conformal prediction), or (b) clearly state that the method offers no formal safety guarantee and reposition it as a heuristic for reducing violations in practice.

- Insufficient justification for the shield horizon and trajectory sampling: The shield horizon H is set to 2 for VGW and 6 for CD, with no explanation for these choices. The paper also samples only N=20 trajectories (vs. 1024 for BPS) but does not analyze how this affects safety coverage. In stochastic environments, a small number of samples can miss rare but catastrophic trajectories, making the shield unreliable. The paper should include an ablation study varying H and N, showing the trade-off between computational cost and safety violations. Without this, the reader cannot assess whether the shield is meaningfully safe or just lucky on the tested environments.

- Limited and potentially biased experimental comparison: The paper compares against only three baselines: unshielded Dreamer, Dreamer+BPS, and CPO. Missing are comparisons to other relevant safe RL methods, particularly those that also use learned models or representations (e.g., model-based safe RL methods like SafeLOOP, or latent-space methods like S3RL mentioned in the literature context). The BPS baseline is also implemented with a discretized action space for the continuous CD environment, which handicaps it. The paper claims superiority but does not control for the number of environment interactions, computational budget, or hyperparameter tuning across methods. A fair comparison would require reporting a Pareto frontier of safety vs. reward for each method, not just point estimates.

- No analysis of the shield's impact on exploration and learning dynamics: The paper introduces a shield introduction schedule to mitigate over-restrictive shielding early in training, but it provides only a brief ablation showing that removing the schedule hurts performance. There is no analysis of how the shield affects exploration, sample efficiency, or convergence. For example, does the shield prevent the agent from discovering high-reward but risky strategies? Does it create a feedback loop where the shield's errors compound with model inaccuracies? These are central concerns for any safe RL method and should be investigated, at least in a small-scale diagnostic environment.

- Incomplete evaluation of generalization and robustness: The paper tests only two environments, one of which (VGW) is deterministic and relatively simple. There is no evaluation on environments with partial observability, multi-agent settings, or distribution shift—despite the paper's motivation being 'high-dimensional' and 'complex' environments. The paper should at least discuss the expected failure modes (e.g., sensor noise, unseen safety-relevant features) and ideally provide experiments on a more challenging benchmark (e.g., a continuous control task with obstacles). Without this, the generality of the approach is unsubstantiated.

- No quantitative bound on real-world safety violations: The paper acknowledges that latent shielding loses formal safety guarantees, but it never quantifies the gap between the learned abstraction and the true environment. Without any bound on the probability of a real-world violation given model error, the reader cannot assess whether the method provides meaningful safety or is merely a heuristic. The paper should either derive a probabilistic safety bound (e.g., using conformal prediction or high-confidence intervals on the SRSSM's predictions) or explicitly reposition the contribution as a heuristic for reducing violations in practice, with a clear statement that no formal guarantee is offered.

- Missing analysis of shield horizon and sample count choices: The shield horizon H is set to 2 for VGW and 6 for CD, and only N=20 trajectories are sampled, yet no ablation or theoretical justification is provided for these values. In stochastic environments, a small sample size can miss rare but catastrophic trajectories, making the shield unreliable. The paper should include an ablation study varying H and N, showing the trade-off between computational cost and safety violations, and discussing how these parameters affect coverage of the trajectory space.

- No comparison to other model-based safe RL methods: The experimental evaluation compares against only three baselines: unshielded Dreamer, Dreamer+BPS, and CPO. Missing are comparisons to other relevant safe RL methods that also use learned models or representations, such as SafeLOOP or latent-space methods like S3RL. The paper claims superiority but does not control for the number of environment interactions, computational budget, or hyperparameter tuning across methods. A fair comparison would require reporting a Pareto frontier of safety vs. reward for each method, not just point estimates.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core idea is promising and the empirical results are suggestive, but the paper lacks the rigorous characterization of safety guarantees, thorough experimental evaluation, and analysis of design choices needed for a top venue. The contribution is not yet clearly established as a reliable method for safe RL.

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal or probabilistic safety guarantee that accounts for model error, or clearly reposition the method as a heuristic without formal guarantees.

- Add ablation studies varying the shield horizon H and number of sampled trajectories N, showing their impact on safety violations and computational cost.

- Expand the experimental comparison to include at least one additional relevant baseline (e.g., a model-based safe RL method or a latent-space safety critic) and report a Pareto frontier of safety vs. reward.

- Analyze the shield's effect on exploration and learning dynamics, including a study of how shield errors propagate during training.

- Test the method on at least one additional environment with different characteristics (e.g., continuous control with obstacles, or a partially observable domain) to demonstrate generality.

comments.title: Abstract claim contradicts paper's own acknowledgment
comments.quote: We experimentally demonstrate that this approach leads to improved adherence to formally-defined safety specifications.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'improved adherence to formally-defined safety specifications,' but the paper later acknowledges that latent shielding loses the formal guarantees of symbolic shielding (Section 3.3.2). The safety specifications are defined over the latent state, not the real environment, so 'adherence' is only with respect to the learned abstraction. This is misleading: a reader would reasonably interpret 'formally-defined safety specifications' as guarantees about the real environment. The abstract should either qualify that the improvement is relative to the learned model or state clearly that no formal safety guarantee is provided.

comments.title: Contradiction between claimed contribution and acknowledged limitation
comments.quote: The main contribution of this work is a framework for shielding agents in complex, stochastic and high-dimensional environments without knowledge of environmental dynamics a priori. We further introduce a new method to aid exploration when training shielded agents. Though our framework loses the formal safety guarantees associated with traditional symbolic shielding approaches, our experiments illustrate that latent shielding reduces unsafe behaviour during training and achieves testing performance comparable to previous symbolic approaches.
comments.explanation: The first sentence claims the framework is for 'shielding agents' — a term that in the safe RL literature implies a mechanism that enforces safety constraints. The third sentence then admits the framework loses formal safety guarantees. This is a direct contradiction: if there are no formal guarantees, the method is not a shield in the standard sense; it is a heuristic for reducing violations. The paper should either (a) define a new, weaker notion of shielding and use it consistently, or (b) replace 'shielding' with a term like 'safety-aware exploration' or 'violation reduction' throughout. Rewrite the first sentence as: 'The main contribution of this work is a framework for reducing safety violations in complex, stochastic and high-dimensional environments without knowledge of environmental dynamics a priori.'

comments.title: Misleading claim about enforcing formal safety specifications
comments.quote: We introduce the notion of latent shielding, a novel class of shielding approaches that replace abstractions used by shields with learned latent dynamics models thus allowing the enforcement of formal safety specifications while avoiding the need for an explicitly-defined abstraction of the environment.
comments.explanation: The claim that latent shielding 'enforces formal safety specifications' is misleading. The paper later acknowledges that the approach loses the formal guarantees of symbolic shielding because the shield operates on a learned latent dynamics model (SRSSM), not the true environment dynamics. A formal safety specification is enforced only if the model perfectly captures the environment, which is never the case in practice. The paper should replace 'enforcing formal safety specifications' with a more accurate phrase such as 'aiming to improve adherence to safety specifications' or 'providing heuristic safety guidance based on a learned model.' This change would align the claim with the actual capabilities of the method and avoid overpromising.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 does not specify how alternative policy selects actions in continuous spaces
comments.quote: our implementation, $\pi_{alt}$ simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found. In the event that no safe action is found, the agent takes a random action.
comments.explanation: The description of π_alt is incomplete for a discrete action space and undefined for a continuous one. 'Considers all the other actions' implies enumeration over a finite action set, but the paper evaluates on the continuous control environment CD (Cheetah Dash) where the action space is continuous. For continuous actions, enumerating 'all other actions' is impossible. The paper must specify how π_alt operates in continuous action spaces — for example, by sampling actions from a uniform distribution over the action space and checking each via the SRSSM until a safe one is found, or by using a gradient-based optimization to find a safe action. This omission makes the algorithm underspecified for the continuous case and undermines the experimental results on CD.

comments.title: Definition of inevitable violation uses wrong safety predicate
comments.quote: we formally define a violation of $\phi$ to be *inevitable* in state $s_{0}\in\mathcal{S}$ if and only if for all $\rho\in\varrho(s_{0})$, $\neg S_{H}(\rho,\phi)$.
comments.explanation: The definition of inevitable violation uses the bounded safety predicate S_H applied to all trajectories in ϱ(s₀), which is the set of all finite trajectories (unbounded). But S_H is defined only for bounded trajectories of length H. Applying S_H to an unbounded trajectory is not well-defined because S_H checks only the first H states, but the definition of S_H as given requires the trajectory to have a specific length structure. The authors likely intend to check whether every bounded prefix of length H from s₀ violates safety, but the notation is incorrect. The fix is to define inevitable violation using bounded trajectories: 'for all ρ ∈ ϱ_H(s₀), ¬S_H(ρ, φ)'. This matches the standard notion in shielding literature where inevitability is checked within the lookahead horizon. Rewrite the definition using ϱ_H(s₀) instead of ϱ(s₀).

comments.title: H-bounded safety definition for policies is incomplete
comments.explanation: The definition of H-bounded safety for a policy has a logical issue. The second condition says 'for all ρ ∈ ϱ_H(s), ¬S_H(ρ, φ)' — meaning no safe trajectory exists from s. In that case, the policy is vacuously considered H-bounded safe regardless of what action it takes. This is problematic because it means a policy that always chooses an unsafe action (when no safe trajectory exists) is still considered safe. The standard definition in shielding literature (e.g., Giacobbe et al.) requires that when a safe trajectory exists, the policy must choose an action that leads to one; when none exists, the policy is allowed to take any action, but the state is labeled as unsafe. The paper's definition conflates 'the policy is safe' with 'the state is unavoidable unsafe'. The definition should be restructured: either separate the notion of policy safety from state safety, or explicitly state that in the second case the policy is not considered safe (or that safety is only defined for states where a safe trajectory exists).

comments.title: Weighted cross-entropy loss has incorrect class weighting
comments.quote:
$l_{\theta}$

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\text {v i o l a t i o n}} = - \frac {1}{| \mathcal {D} _ {\lambda} |} \sum_ {\left((h, z), \lambda\right) \in \mathcal {D} _ {\lambda}} \left(\alpha \lambda \log \left(l _ {\theta} (h, z)\right) + (1 - \alpha) (1 - \lambda) \log \left(1 - l _ {\theta} (h, z)\right)\right) \tag {3}
$$

where  $\mat
comments.explanation: The weighting scheme in Equation (3) is not correctly normalized. The term α multiplies only the unsafe-class loss, and (1−α) multiplies only the safe-class loss. This means the total loss contribution from a safe example is (1−α) and from an unsafe example is α, but these are not normalized by the class proportions in the dataset. The standard approach for class-imbalanced binary cross-entropy is to set weights inversely proportional to class frequencies, e.g., w_0 = N / (2 * N_0) for safe and w_1 = N / (2 * N_1) for unsafe, where N_0 and N_1 are the counts of safe and unsafe examples. The current formulation with a single α cannot correctly handle arbitrary imbalance ratios: if unsafe examples are rare, α should be large, but then (1−α) becomes small, potentially underweighting the safe class. The authors should either use per-class weights derived from class frequencies or clarify that α is tuned as a hyperparameter and show that it is sufficient for the tested environments.

comments.title: Equation (4) uses undefined probability notation
comments.explanation: The notation P(l_θ(s_{t+1})=1 | π(s_t)) is ambiguous. First, s_{t+1} is not defined in terms of the SRSSM dynamics — it is unclear whether this probability marginalizes over the stochastic transitions of the RSSM or over the true environment. Second, conditioning on π(s_t) alone does not specify a distribution over s_{t+1}; one must also condition on the current state s_t and the transition model. The intended meaning is likely the probability that the labelling function predicts an unsafe state at the next time step given that action a = π(s_t) is taken from state s_t under the learned model. Rewrite the condition as P(l_θ(s') = 1 | s_t, a = π(s_t)) where s' ~ p_θ(· | s_t, a) and p_θ is the SRSSM transition distribution. This makes the probabilistic object well-defined and matches the sampling procedure in Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of violation conflates state label with trajectory satisfaction
comments.quote: We assume the environment has been augmented with a labelling function $L_{\mathcal{E}}^{\phi}:\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{E}}\rightarrow\{safe,unsafe\}$ that, at each time step, informs us whether a violation has occurred with respect to some formal safety specification $\phi$. For the avoidance of doubt, we define a violation to have occurred whenever $\phi$ does not hold.
comments.explanation: The labelling function maps states to {safe, unsafe}, but the text then defines a violation as occurring whenever φ does not hold. This conflates the state-level label with the specification-level truth value. In sc-LTL, φ is evaluated over a trajectory, not a single state. A state may be labeled 'unsafe' even if φ has not yet been violated (e.g., entering a dangerous region before a deadline), and conversely a violation may occur without the current state being labeled unsafe (e.g., a temporal condition fails due to past actions). The paper should clarify whether L_Ε^φ labels states based on the current satisfaction of φ (which is ill-defined for temporal formulas) or based on a state-wise safety predicate. If the latter, the definition of violation should be tied to the trajectory-level satisfaction of φ, not to the state label. Rewrite the definition to separate the state-level safety predicate from the specification-level violation condition.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about high-dimensional environments
comments.quote: We present a novel approach to safety-aware deep reinforcement learning in high-dimensional environments called latent shielding.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the method works in 'high-dimensional environments,' yet the experimental evaluation (Section 4) only tests on two environments: a grid world (VGW) and a continuous control task (CD). Neither is high-dimensional in the sense typically used in deep RL (e.g., pixel-based observations, large state spaces). The paper does not demonstrate the method on any environment with high-dimensional observations (e.g., images, lidar). This claim is unsupported by the evidence presented. The authors should either provide an experiment on a genuinely high-dimensional environment or remove the claim.

comments.title: Misattribution of empirical finding to Giacobbe et al.
comments.quote: Under this definition, however, it has been found that many state-of-the-art RL algorithms unnecessarily enter unsafe states despite safe alternatives being available and there being a positive correlation between avoiding such states and reward *(Giacobbe et al., 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The cited reference (Giacobbe et al., 2021) does not make this claim. That paper proposes a model-based shielding method and evaluates it on grid-world and continuous control tasks; it does not conduct a broad empirical study of 'many state-of-the-art RL algorithms' nor does it report a positive correlation between safety and reward across algorithms. The claim appears to be a general observation that the authors are attributing to a single source without evidence. Either remove the citation and state this as a motivation (which is fine for an introduction) or replace it with a reference that actually supports the claim, such as a survey on safe RL (e.g., García & Fernández, 2015).

comments.title: Incorrect characterization of shielding's formal verifiability
comments.quote: A key advantage of many shielding approaches is that the resulting shielded policies are formally verifiable; however, a shortcoming is that they require a model of environmental dynamics - typically handcrafted - to be provided in advance.
comments.explanation: The claim that shielded policies are 'formally verifiable' conflates two distinct concepts. In the shielding literature (e.g., Alshiekh et al., 2018), the shield itself is formally verified against a given model, but the combined agent+shield policy is not formally verified in the sense of guaranteeing safety in the real environment—it is only verified with respect to the provided model. The paper's own later discussion acknowledges that latent shielding loses formal guarantees because the model is learned. The introduction should be precise: the shield's action filter is formally correct relative to the model, but the overall policy's safety in the real environment depends on model accuracy. Rewrite to: 'A key advantage of many shielding approaches is that the shield's decisions are formally correct with respect to the provided model; however, a shortcoming is that they require a model of environmental dynamics...'

comments.title: Overly broad claim about reducing training violations
comments.quote: In addition, the agent can be trained within its own latent world model thus reducing the number of safety violations seen during training.
comments.explanation: This claim is misleading. Training within the latent world model does not reduce safety violations during training in the real environment—it eliminates them entirely during model-based rollouts, but the agent still interacts with the real environment (e.g., to collect data for model learning). The paper's own algorithm (Section 3.3.2) shows that the agent collects real environment experience and applies the shield during real interactions. The introduction should clarify that model-based training reduces violations during imagined rollouts, not during real-environment training. Rewrite to: 'In addition, the agent can be trained within its own latent world model, reducing the number of safety violations that would otherwise occur during real-environment exploration.'

comments.title: Incorrect claim about classical shielding horizon
comments.quote: This is not the case for classical shielding which resembles BPS with an infinite horizon.
comments.explanation: The claim that classical shielding 'resembles BPS with an infinite horizon' is incorrect. Classical shielding (Alshiekh et al., 2018) constructs a reactive system from a finite-state abstraction and an LTL specification, which provides a safety guarantee for all future steps — but this is not equivalent to BPS with an infinite lookahead horizon. BPS with an infinite horizon would require simulating the environment forward infinitely many steps, which is not what classical shielding does. Classical shielding uses a precomputed safety game on the abstraction, not forward simulation. The comparison conflates two different mechanisms. Rewrite the sentence as: 'This is not the case for classical shielding, which provides a safety guarantee for all future steps via a precomputed safety game on the abstraction, rather than by forward simulation.'

comments.title: Definition of bounded trajectory length is inconsistent
comments.quote: *bounded trajectory* $\rho$ of length $H$ is a sequence of states and actions $s_{0}\xrightarrow{a_{0}}s_{1}\xrightarrow{a_{1}}\ldots\xrightarrow{a_{n-1}}s_{n}$ comprised of no more than $H$ states and with the final state $s_{n}$ either being a terminal state or $n=H-1$.
comments.explanation: The definition of a bounded trajectory of length H is ambiguous. The text says the trajectory has 'no more than H states' and that the final state satisfies either being terminal or n = H-1. If n = H-1, then the trajectory has exactly H states (indices 0 through H-1). But if the trajectory terminates early, it has fewer than H states. The condition 'n = H-1' is also inconsistent with the earlier statement that the trajectory is 'of length H' — if n = H-1, the trajectory has H states, but the phrase 'no more than H states' allows fewer. The definition should be clarified: either a bounded trajectory always has exactly H states (with early termination handled by absorbing states), or the length parameter H should be defined as the maximum number of steps. The current wording is self-contradictory. Rewrite the definition to be unambiguous: e.g., 'a bounded trajectory of length H is a sequence of exactly H states and H-1 actions, where the final state may be terminal (in which case subsequent states are undefined).'

comments.title: Figure 1 caption mislabels the violation prediction variable
comments.quote: Figure 1: Safety RSSM. In this example, the model observes the environment for two time steps and predicts the subsequent two time steps.  $a_{t}$  denotes the action taken at step  $t$ ,  $o_{t}$  the observation,  $h_{t}$  the deterministic component of the latent state,  $z_{t}$  the posterior stochastic component,  $\hat{z}_{t}$  the prior stochastic component,  $\hat{\lambda}_{t}$  the violation prediction (our novel contribution, highlighted in yellow),  $\hat{o}_{t}$  the predicted observation and  $\hat{r}_{t}$  the predicted reward.
comments.explanation: The caption states that $\hat{\lambda}_{t}$ is the violation prediction, but the variable name uses a hat over lambda, which in the paper's notation (see Section 3.3.2) denotes the predicted safety label from the learned classifier. However, the figure shows $\hat{\lambda}_{t}$ as a stochastic variable (circle), while the safety label $\lambda_t$ is binary and deterministic given the state. The paper elsewhere treats $\lambda_t$ as a deterministic function of the state (the safety specification), not a stochastic prediction. This inconsistency between the figure's stochastic representation and the deterministic nature of the safety label is confusing. The authors should either (a) clarify that $\hat{\lambda}_{t}$ is a stochastic prediction from the RSSM's classifier (which would be appropriate if the classifier outputs a probability), or (b) change the circle to a square to indicate determinism. Rewrite the caption to explain that $\hat{\lambda}_{t}$ is the predicted safety label (a stochastic variable when the classifier is probabilistic) and ensure the figure's shape matches the intended semantics.

comments.title: Vague description of Dreamer modification
comments.quote: In this work, our model-based agent of choice is Dreamer (Hafner et al., 2021) $^{1}$ , which we modify to incorporate shielding into its data collection, training and deployment phases.
comments.explanation: The description of how Dreamer is modified is too vague. The reader is told that shielding is incorporated into 'data collection, training and deployment phases,' but no specifics are given about what changes are made to Dreamer's architecture, loss functions, or training loop. This lack of detail makes it impossible to understand the contribution or reproduce the method. The paper should provide a concise summary of the key modifications (e.g., how the shield's output affects the reward signal, how the latent state is used for lookahead, and how the shield introduction schedule works) in this introductory section, with a reference to the detailed description in Section 3.

comments.title: Labelling function output is categorical but loss uses logits
comments.quote: $L_{\mathcal{I}}^{\phi}: S_{\mathcal{I}} \to \{safe, unsafe\}$  which maps latent states  $s_t \in S_{\mathcal{I}}$  to whether they correspond to states in violation of  $\phi$ . As with the other models,  $L_{\mathcal{I}}^{\phi}$  is implemented with a neural network with a categorical output  $l_{\theta}$  also parameterised by  $\theta$ .
comments.explanation: The description says the labelling function has a categorical output {safe, unsafe}, but the loss in Equation (3) uses l_θ(h,z) as a scalar probability (since it is passed to log and 1−l_θ). If the output is categorical (e.g., a one-hot vector), then l_θ(h,z) would be a vector, and the log would be ill-defined. The authors likely mean that the network outputs a single scalar probability (via a sigmoid activation) representing the probability of being unsafe. The text should be corrected to say 'a scalar output in [0,1]' or 'a Bernoulli distribution parameter' to match the loss function.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses undefined compact latent state notation
comments.quote: Input: Current compact latent state $(h,z)$, unshielded action $a$, horizon $H$, number of trajectories to sample $N$, alternative policy $\pi_{alt}$ and threshold $\epsilon$.
comments.explanation: The notation (h,z) for the compact latent state is introduced here without prior definition. Earlier in the paper (Section 3.1), the SRSSM is described as having a deterministic recurrent component h_t and a stochastic component z_t, but the pair (h,z) is never formally defined as the 'compact latent state.' Moreover, the algorithm uses (h,z) as the starting state for trajectory imagination, but the RSSM typically conditions rollouts on the full belief state (h_t, z_t) along with the action. The paper should explicitly state that the compact latent state is the concatenation (h_t, z_t) and clarify how the initial (h,z) is obtained from the encoder at the current time step. Without this, the reader cannot reproduce the shielding procedure.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of dataset D
comments.quote: the agent interacts with the real environment to collect a dataset $\mathcal{D}\subseteq\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}\times\mathcal{O}\times\mathbb{R}\times\{0,1\}$ of states, actions, observations, rewards and violations with which a latent world model can be learned.
comments.explanation: The dataset D is defined as a subset of the Cartesian product S × A × O × R × {0,1}, which suggests each entry is a 5-tuple (s, a, o, r, λ). However, Algorithm 2 (line 22, quoted in the cross-reference) stores tuples (o_t, a_t, r_t, λ_t) — a 4-tuple without the state s. The definition in the text includes states, but the algorithm omits them. This inconsistency matters because the world model training (Equation 2) likely requires observations, not states. Either the definition should match the algorithm (remove S from the product) or the algorithm should be corrected to include states. Rewrite the definition as D ⊆ A × O × R × {0,1} (or include states in the algorithm) to resolve the mismatch.

comments.title: Inconsistent update of experience dataset in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: 22 Add experience to dataset  $\mathcal{D} := \mathcal{D} \cup \{(o_t, a_t, r_t, \lambda_t)_{t=1}^T\}$ .
comments.explanation: Line 22 adds the entire episode's data at once after the inner loop, but the loop at line 10 already runs for t=1..T and each step should be added immediately (as is standard in RL). The notation $(o_t, a_t, r_t, \lambda_t)_{t=1}^T$ suggests a single tuple containing all time steps, which is inconsistent with the incremental collection. The fix is to move the append inside the loop, e.g., 'Add experience $(o_t, a_t, r_t, \lambda_t)$ to $\mathcal{D}$' after line 20 or 21.

comments.title: Missing subscript in data sequence sampling
comments.quote: 4 Draw  $B$  data sequences  $\{(a_t, o_t, r_t, \lambda_t)_{t=k}^k\} \sim \mathcal{D}$ .
comments.explanation: The notation $(a_t, o_t, r_t, \lambda_t)_{t=k}^k$ is ambiguous: the index runs from k to k, meaning each sequence has length 1, which contradicts the need for sequences of length L (as stated in the input parameters). The intended notation is likely $(a_t, o_t, r_t, \lambda_t)_{t=k}^{k+L-1}$ or similar, to indicate a subsequence of length L. This should be corrected to match the standard Dreamer training procedure.

comments.title: Unclear handling of shield interference reward override
comments.quote:
20 if  $\lambda_t = 1$  or interfered then  $r_t := r_{\text{punish}}$ .
21 end
comments.explanation: The reward override in line 20 applies the punishment whenever the environment detects a violation OR the shield interfered. This means the agent is punished even when the shield prevents a violation (interfered but no actual violation). This conflates two distinct events: a prevented violation and an actual violation. The punishment should only apply when a violation actually occurs (lambda_t=1), not when the shield merely corrects an action. Overriding reward on interference may discourage the agent from learning to avoid states that trigger the shield, which is counterproductive. The fix is to change the condition to 'if lambda_t = 1 then r_t := r_punish'.

comments.title: Shield introduction schedule not used in Algorithm 2
comments.quote:
13 if  $\Pi$  has enabled shielding then
14 Pass  $h_t, z_t$  and  $a_t$  into Algorithm 1 to obtain the tuple  $\langle a_t', \text{interfered} \rangle$ .
15 else
16  $a_t', \text{interfered} := a_t$ , false.
17 end
comments.explanation: The shield introduction schedule $\Pi$ is listed as an input to Algorithm 2 but its behavior is not defined. The text says 'if $\Pi$ has enabled shielding' but does not specify how $\Pi$ decides this. The schedule is described in the text as a function that gradually introduces shielding, but the algorithm gives no mechanism for how $\Pi$ determines whether shielding is enabled at a given time step. This makes the algorithm underspecified. The fix is to define $\Pi$ as a function of training step or episode number, e.g., $\Pi(t)$ returning a boolean, and clarify in the algorithm that the check is based on the current training progress.

comments.title: Table reference mismatch: Table 4 vs Table 1
comments.quote: As can be seen in Table 4, our agent collected more reward at test-time than both the unshielded and BPS agents in the VGW environment
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Table 4' but the table immediately above the quoted passage is labeled 'Table 1'. This is an internal cross-reference error. Readers will be confused about which table contains the reported results. The authors should change 'Table 4' to 'Table 1' to match the actual table label in the section.

comments.title: Claim of seven-fold reduction in violations not supported by data
comments.quote: our agent saw a seven-fold reduction in test-time violations compared to the unshielded agent across both the fixed and procedurally generated environments, averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 'seven-fold reduction' is ambiguous and not directly verifiable from the table. For the fixed VGW environment, the unshielded agent has 2.25 violations per episode and the latent agent has 0.30 — that is a 7.5-fold reduction, which matches. However, for the procedural VGW, the unshielded agent has 33.7 violations and the latent agent has 4.50 — that is a 7.5-fold reduction as well. But the text says 'across both the fixed and procedurally generated environments, averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW.' The phrase 'averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW' is correct for the fixed case (0.30) but the procedural case averages 4.50 violations per episode, which is not less than one. The wording conflates the two environments. The authors should rephrase to state the reduction factor separately for each environment and note that only the fixed VGW achieves sub-one violations.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of Giacobbe et al. 2021
comments.quote: To our knowledge, few works *(Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Giacobbe et al., 2021)* focus on removing the need for handcrafting an abstraction (among the most time-consuming and error-prone aspects of shielding), and only one of them *(Giacobbe et al., 2021)* achieves this without getting rid of the abstraction altogether, albeit by providing the agent with access to the program that controls the environment.
comments.explanation: This claim about Giacobbe et al. (2021) is inaccurate. That work does not 'remove the need for handcrafting an abstraction' — it still requires a manually specified safety specification (a set of unsafe states) and a programmatic environment model. The paper does not learn an abstraction from raw observations; it assumes access to the environment's internal program, which is itself a form of handcrafted abstraction. The cited work therefore does not achieve the claimed feat of eliminating handcrafted abstractions, and the paper's positioning of latent shielding as uniquely addressing this limitation is overstated.

comments.title: Overstated uniqueness of learned abstraction approach
comments.quote: In contrast, latent shielding tackles the problem head-on by directly learning an abstraction for use by the shield.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that latent shielding is unique in 'directly learning an abstraction for use by the shield,' but this ignores prior work that also learns abstractions for shielding. For example, Srinivasan et al. (2020) and Thananjeyan et al. (2021) learn safety constraints from data without handcrafted abstractions, and Bharadhwaj et al. (2021) learns latent safety representations. The paper's own citation list includes these works, yet the text implies they 'get rid of the abstraction altogether,' which conflates 'no explicit symbolic abstraction' with 'no learned abstraction.' The distinction should be clarified to avoid misrepresenting prior work.

comments.title: Overclaiming formal safety guarantee loss
comments.quote: our realisation of latent shielding loses the formally verifiable safety guarantees enjoyed by many symbolic shielding approaches - whether it is possible to construct a verifiable implementation of latent shielding, or compensate for the loss of formal guarantees, are open problems.
comments.explanation: The paper's technical sections never establish that symbolic shielding approaches actually provide 'formally verifiable safety guarantees' in the tested environments. The BPS baseline, which is the symbolic shielding method compared against, is implemented with a discretized action space for the continuous environment, and the paper does not verify that its abstraction is sound or complete. Claiming that symbolic shielding 'enjoys' formal guarantees without demonstrating that the BPS implementation actually satisfies those guarantees in the tested settings is misleading. The paper should either (a) provide evidence that the BPS baseline's abstraction is formally verified for the environments used, or (b) rephrase the sentence to avoid implying that the comparison baseline has a property that was not actually verified in the experiments.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of BPS applicability
comments.quote: works out-of-the-box on both continuous and discrete environments (unlike its predecessor, BPS)
comments.explanation: The paper's own experimental section shows that BPS was adapted to continuous environments by discretizing the action space. This is a modification, not a fundamental limitation of BPS. The claim that BPS does not work 'out-of-the-box' on continuous environments is misleading because BPS is designed for discrete action spaces, and the paper does not test BPS in its native setting (discrete actions) on the continuous environment. The comparison is unfair because BPS is used in a modified form that may not reflect its intended performance. The paper should either (a) compare BPS in its native discrete-action setting on a discrete environment, or (b) acknowledge that the comparison is between latent shielding and an adapted version of BPS, not the original method.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of safety specification integration
comments.quote: An RSSM is composed of three key components: a latent dynamics model, a reward model, and an observation model. These components act on compact states formed from the concatenation of a deterministic latent state $h_{t}$ and stochastic latent state $z_{t}$.
comments.explanation: The section describes the RSSM components but does not explain how the safety specification φ (introduced in Section 2.1) is integrated into this model. The paper's main contribution is safety-aware RL via latent shielding, yet the methodology section omits any mention of how the safety-relevant features are extracted from the latent state or how the labeling function λ_t is defined in terms of h_t and z_t. Without this connection, the reader cannot assess whether the latent representation is sufficient for safety verification. The authors should add a paragraph after the RSSM description that formally defines the labeling function λ_t = f(h_t, z_t) and explains how the safety specification φ is encoded in the latent space.

comments.title: Missing definition of compact state z_t
comments.quote: First, the recurrent model $h_{t}=f(h_{t-1},z_{t-1},a_{t-1})$ is used to compute the deterministic latent state based on the previous compact state and action. From $h_{t}$ and the current observation $o_{t}$
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'the previous compact state' as $z_{t-1}$, but $z_t$ has not been defined yet. The reader is left to infer that $z_t$ is the stochastic latent state introduced in the next sentence. However, the phrase 'compact state' is ambiguous — it could refer to the deterministic state $h_t$, the stochastic state $z_t$, or a concatenation of both. This forward reference creates confusion about the input to the recurrent model. Rewrite the first sentence to define $z_t$ before using it: 'First, the recurrent model $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, z_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$ computes the deterministic latent state, where $z_{t-1}$ is the stochastic latent state from the previous time step (defined below).'

comments.title: Incomplete sentence after observation reference
comments.quote: From $h_{t}$ and the current observation $o_{t}$
comments.explanation: The sentence beginning 'From $h_t$ and the current observation $o_t$' is cut off by a page break and never completed. The next sentence starts with 'distribution $q(z_t|h_t,o_t)$ over posterior stochastic latent states $z_t$ is computed by the *representation model*.' This leaves a grammatical fragment that obscures the logical flow. The authors should complete the sentence, e.g., 'From $h_t$ and the current observation $o_t$, a distribution $q(z_t|h_t,o_t)$ over posterior stochastic latent states $z_t$ is computed by the *representation model*.'

comments.title: Missing formal definition of the latent MDP state space
comments.quote: These latent dynamics models define a fully-observable *Markov decision process (MDP)* as the latent states in the agent’s own internal model can always be observed by the agent *(Hafner et al., 2019a)*. We denote the state space of this MDP (comprised of compact latent states) as $\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{I}}$.
comments.explanation: The text defines the latent MDP state space as 'comprised of compact latent states' but never specifies what constitutes a 'compact latent state.' From the preceding description, the latent state appears to be the pair $(h_t, z_t)$, where $h_t$ is deterministic and $z_t$ is stochastic. However, the paper later uses $s_t$ to denote the latent state (e.g., in the shield definition), and it is unclear whether $s_t$ includes both $h_t$ and $z_t$ or just one of them. This ambiguity propagates to the shield's transition and safety prediction. The authors should provide an explicit definition: e.g., 'We define the latent state as $s_t = (h_t, z_t) \in \mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{I}}$, where $h_t$ is the deterministic recurrent state and $z_t$ is the stochastic latent state.'

comments.title: Qualitative claim lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: Results can be seen in Appendix G and indicate that, qualitatively, our agent's decoder performed the best, accurately predicting 10 frames into the future. One possible reason for this observation is that the inclusion of the violation detection objective in the SRSSM encourages the model to focus on accurately capturing violation states. Another potential factor is that the BPS agent never actually enters unsafe states and thus finds it difficult to represent them.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'our agent's decoder performed the best' is based on a qualitative inspection of decoded frames, but no quantitative metric (e.g., mean squared error, structural similarity index, or prediction accuracy) is reported. Without a numerical comparison, the reader cannot verify that the difference is meaningful or statistically significant. The two proposed explanations are speculative and not tested. Add a quantitative comparison of prediction error (e.g., MSE over the 10-step horizon) across agents, with standard errors over multiple starting states and action sequences.

comments.title: Ablation result reported without quantitative detail
comments.quote: We perform an ablation study with shield introduction schedules in the VGW environment. Results can be found in Appendix I and demonstrate decreased performance in the absence of shield introduction schedules.
comments.explanation: The text states that performance decreases without shield introduction schedules, but it does not specify which metric(s) decreased (e.g., cumulative reward, number of violations, or both) or by how much. The reader cannot assess the practical significance of the schedule. Report the mean and standard error of both safety and reward metrics for the with-schedule and without-schedule conditions, ideally over multiple random seeds.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about explainability advantage
comments.quote: Moreover, latent shielding may provide a greater degree of explainability over model-free methods which get rid of the abstraction altogether *(Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021)* - if the shield overrides an action, one can reconstruct the imagined unsafe trajectories that led to the interference.
comments.explanation: The claim that latent shielding offers 'greater explainability' over model-free methods is not supported by any evidence or analysis in the paper. The cited model-free methods (Srinivasan et al., Thananjeyan et al., Bharadhwaj et al.) may also provide interpretability through learned safety critics or constraint violations. Moreover, the paper does not demonstrate that the 'imagined unsafe trajectories' from the latent world model are actually interpretable to humans, nor does it compare the interpretability of its approach to any baseline. This is an unsubstantiated qualitative claim that should be removed or supported with evidence.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated novelty claim for latent shielding
comments.quote: We further introduce the first such approach, approximate bounded prescience shielding, a framework for latent shielding that leverages latent world models learnt by model-based deep RL (DRL) agents.
comments.explanation: The claim that this is the 'first such approach' to latent shielding is not substantiated. The paper does not provide a literature review demonstrating that no prior work has combined learned latent dynamics models with shielding. Given the existence of related methods like SafeLOOP and S3RL (mentioned in the literature context), the authors should either provide evidence that no prior work exists or temper the claim to 'a novel approach' or 'one of the first approaches.' Without this, the novelty assertion is unverifiable and risks being inaccurate.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about out-of-the-box operation
comments.quote: we have demonstrated for the first time that shielding at inappropriate times may adversely impact the performance of model-based DRL agents and showed how this phenomenon can be counteracted using our novel notion of *shield introduction schedules*.
comments.explanation: The claim that the paper 'demonstrated for the first time' that shielding at inappropriate times may adversely impact performance is not supported by the experimental results. The paper only tests two environments and does not provide a systematic analysis of when shielding is inappropriate or how the shield introduction schedule counteracts the adverse impact. The ablation study on the schedule is minimal (only one variant tested). To substantiate this claim, the paper would need to show that the schedule consistently improves performance across multiple environments and safety specifications, and that the adverse impact of shielding is a general phenomenon, not an artifact of the specific environments tested.

comments.title: Missing description of shield application during data collection
comments.quote: At the very start of training, we collect trajectories from $S$ seed episodes using a random policy; at all other times, we use the agent’s shielded policy.
comments.explanation: The text states that after seed episodes, the agent uses its 'shielded policy' for data collection. However, the shield (ABPS) is constructed from the latent world model, which is only trained after seed episodes. The paper does not explain how the shield is initialized or applied during the first few cycles of data collection after seed episodes. If the shield is not yet available, the agent would use an unshielded policy, contradicting the claim. The authors should clarify the initialization of the shield and whether there is a warm-up period before shielding begins.

comments.title: Unclear whether observation model is used in shield
comments.quote: Though not strictly needed, the observation model can prove useful for visualising predicted future states and providing a richer training signal.
comments.explanation: The paper states the observation model is 'not strictly needed' but does not clarify whether it is actually used in the latent shielding procedure. If the shield relies on the latent dynamics model to predict future safety violations, and the observation model is omitted during shield construction, the reader cannot assess whether the shield's predictions are based solely on the latent state or also on reconstructed observations. This ambiguity matters because the observation model could provide additional signal for detecting safety-relevant features not captured in the latent state. The authors should explicitly state whether the observation model is used during shield rollouts and, if not, justify why its omission does not degrade safety prediction.

comments.title: Incomplete description of the Cliff Driver environment
comments.quote: Cliff Driver The Cliff Driver (CD) environment is a symbolic benchmark with stochastic dynamics and continuous actions. The agent controls the forward acceleration of a car and is tasked with driving to the edge of a cliff as quickly as possible without overshooting and falling into the sea. Stochasticity comes from the fact that at each time step there is a probability  $p_{stick}$  that car's controls become "stuck" (Machado et al., 2018).
